You have to know when to bring in help. You see, in life we don't know what we don't know. There's a famous CIA video that I'll actually try to cut into the video now. And what the gentleman says is this, you don't know what you don't know. This is how he explains it. He gets up on stage, he's in front of a press crowd, and the press is like sitting back on the edge of their seats, ready to hear what the CIA commissioner, I think it was Bush, was saying at the time, he goes, See in life, there are known knowns, which are things in which we know that we know. There are known unknowns, which are things that we know that we do not know. And there are also unknown unknowns, which are things in which we don't know that we do not know. And then somebody in the press crowd jokes around, and they were like, is this an unknown unknown or a known known? All that being said, this is a video to help you identify that in life, there are a lot of unknown unknowns, and there are also some known unknowns. Known unknowns are cool because we at least identify that they hold us back. Unknown unknowns are when we need to know that we need to bring in help, even though we don't know, technically we need to bring in help. And I'm going to explain that logic. You need to know when you've hit a wall, you need to know that you can't deliver in a certain area or can't educate your team in a certain area, or that you can't do something. You need to bring in help when those times occur. You need to understand that self-educating and educating your team and bringing in consultants and consulting industry, being over billions of dollars generated every year inside of it, with dollars for you too that I'll teach you how to sell, is something that is there for a reason. There are all types of experts and specialists who literally identify with every single problem in existence. Have you ever heard or seen one of those funky videos in school where you're watching in Home Ec, for example, a video where they're talking about cheese, and all of a sudden, there's an expert that comes on the screen where literally their job title is something so detailed and specific that it just kind of baffled you that it's an actual job. Like, they're the 1989 cheese expert only in southern Italy. Like, that's like their job title. There are niche experts in every single thing ever, and you just need to know when to bring them in. Now, a lot of times, coaches, experts, people who make you better, people who you have to invest into to show you things, especially through information, tangible or intangible, direct one-on-one coach or group coach or digital or you don't even get access to anything other than videos, you need to understand that that's great that you've brought in help to that point. But you've also got to understand that those people may be expensive. One of the hardest things to expense is an expense on information products. Most of us, unless highly seeking a desired result, kind of struggle with the investment into a coach or a mentor. I know I did when I first started paying $1,000 a month to have a business coach talk to me for one hour a week, each week, and I've been doing that consistently, and I've raised the cost to now teach and coach other people inside of my team too, that's how much I justify it now, for over $2,000 a month over the last eight months. That's helped my business more than almost anything else from having somebody who I can talk to who's a non-biased coach, who's that person in my ear for one hour each week, that I get to essentially knock my problems off of who directly tailors a response to help me in that exact moment to overcome my specific issues that I'm having in business. Greatest assets that I've ever had. One of the greatest assets I would tell is to help and pass on to you. I didn't even refer to the person who does it for me. His name's Graham Payne. I love everything that he does. He's taught me about conditions and some of the other overall business logic that I've passed on to you guys. But being said, you have to know when to bring in help. Alls I knew eight months ago was that I needed some general coaching in business because at the time I was 22, I just turned 23 on April 30th. My business was flourishing, but I knew that I was limited because I just didn't know what I didn't know. I wanted somebody else to come in and literally just look at everything I was doing, my success, all my failures, every single thing. And I wanted to show them because I wanted to get a perspective from somebody who's built several eight-figure businesses and exited from them and one nine-figure business and exited from it. It is now coaching people because that's how much he cares about results that others generate in business. That's my passion. It's finding other people who are willing to help other people. That's what I do personality brand development for. I know when to seek out other people. You may not, and that's why I make this video. Operations can be a bitch sometimes. I had months where I had to fire nine people at a time and bring in a whole new team of creative people because my team wasn't performing in cancer and spread throughout that part of that organization. You have to know when to bring in help. You have to know when you need assistance. Operations are one of the most important parts of your entire business. They are your business. It's the whole premise of you being able to go and sell anything in the market from your ability to create smooth operations that people go through that are systematic where people are held accountable where results are delivered and derived consistently in a way where you flourish and have fluency and a stress-free environment doing it. You need to know when to bring in help.
